Patchwork Analysis

Kaia Newman

04-11-2026

install.packages("simr")
trying URL 'https://cran.rstudio.com/bin/macosx/big-sur-arm64/contrib/4.3/simr_1.0.7.tgz'
Content type 'application/x-gzip' length 176116 bytes (171 KB)

downloaded 171 KB

The downloaded binary packages are in
    /var/folders/4p/mbgtwhns405crk_pd0vnvd3c0000gn/T//Rtmp7gDZtF/downloaded_packages

Load libraries

#load libraries
library(ggplot2)
library(dplyr)
library(lme4)
library(psych)
library(multcomp)
library(parameters)
library(performance)
library(car)

Read in analysis dataframe and preprocess columns

# Make full df, gaze-valid df, full df had patch, gaze-valid had patch 
full_df = read.csv("timing_correctness_data.csv")

# Make boolean columns
full_df$correct = ifelse(full_df$correct == "Y", TRUE, FALSE)
full_df$had_patch = (full_df$condition == "overfitting" | full_df$condition == "correct")
# P2 t3 was cut off 5 minutes early
# make column for that, add to model, remove if not explanatory of much variance
full_df$cut_off = FALSE
full_df$cut_off[full_df$PID == 'P2' & full_df$task_no == 3] = TRUE

# Make other random effects factors
full_df$PID = as.factor(full_df$PID)
full_df$bug = as.factor(full_df$bug)
full_df$condition = as.factor(full_df$condition)

# make numPrev column
full_df$num_prev = full_df$task_no - 1
full_df$num_prev = as.factor(full_df$num_prev)

# For IDK in would_submit category, turn to NA
full_df$would_submit[full_df$would_submit == 'IDK'] = NA
# Also, blanks got read in as "" instead of NA
full_df$would_submit[full_df$would_submit == ""] = NA
full_df$think_patch_correct[full_df$think_patch_correct == ""] = NA
# Combine Y/P for self-reported data since they have the same valence
full_df$think_correct[full_df$think_correct == 'Y' | full_df$think_correct == 'P'] = 'YP'
full_df$think_patch_correct[full_df$think_patch_correct == 'Y' | full_df$think_patch_correct == 'P'] = 'YP'
full_df$would_submit[full_df$would_submit == 'Y' | full_df$would_submit == 'P'] = 'YP'

# Make columns for correct patch/fix judgments 
full_df$accurate_about_correctness = (full_df$correct == TRUE & full_df$think_correct == 'YP') | 
                                      (full_df$correct == FALSE & full_df$think_correct == 'N')
full_df$accurate_about_patch_correctness = (full_df$correct == TRUE & full_df$think_correct == 'YP') | 
                                      (full_df$correct == FALSE & full_df$think_correct == 'N')

Prep data from survey to put in analysis dataframe

# Add columns for in-study survey per participant-task
survey = read.csv("patchwork_survey.csv")

# Find duplicate PIDs and rename to P1-0 through P6-0
which(tolower(survey$Q24) == "p1")
[1]  4 10
survey$Q24[4] = "P1_0"
survey$Q24[5] = "P2_0"
survey$Q24[6] = "P3_0"
survey$Q24[7] = "P4_0"
survey$Q24[8] = "P5_0"
survey$Q24[9] = "P6_0"

# Q24 -- PID
# Q1_10 -- mental/perceptual activity
# Q2_1 -- time pressure
# Q3_1 -- performance
# Q4_1 -- effort
# Q5_1 -- frustration

# and so on for t2:
# Q44_10 -- mental/perceptual activity
# Q45_1 -- time pressure
# ...

# and for t3:
# Q50_10 -- mental/perceptual activity
# Q51_1 -- time pressure
# ...

# Create average cognitive load for t1, 2, and 3
data.frame(index = seq_along(survey), name = names(survey))
survey$Q24 = as.factor(survey$Q24)
# some people did not drag the scale and meant 0 -- change NaNs to 0. more accurate to what really happened than missing data
fill = function(x) replace(as.numeric(x), is.na(as.numeric(x)), 0)
survey[, 20:24] = lapply(survey[, 20:24], fill)
survey[, 26:30] = lapply(survey[, 26:30], fill)
survey[, 32:36] = lapply(survey[, 32:36], fill)


# level of performance (Q3_1, Q46_1, Q52_1) should be inverse
survey$Q3_1 = 21 - survey$Q3_1
survey$Q46_1 = 21 - survey$Q46_1
survey$Q52_1 = 21 - survey$Q52_1

survey$t1_cognitive_load = rowMeans(survey[, 20:24])
survey$t2_cognitive_load = rowMeans(survey[, 26:30])
survey$t3_cognitive_load = rowMeans(survey[, 32:36])

# Now set ONE column in timing_data which has these averages, matching with PID and task number
lookup = as.matrix(survey[, c("t1_cognitive_load", "t2_cognitive_load", "t3_cognitive_load")])
rownames(lookup) = survey$Q24

full_df$cognitive_load = lookup[
  cbind(
    match(full_df$PID, rownames(lookup)),
    full_df$task_no
  )
]

# TODO: maybe OCEAN personality data, but that's not on the critical path

# Chop off header in survey
survey = survey[4:nrow(survey), ]

# rename column to make easier to remember as predictor
survey = rename(survey, PID = Q24)

# Subset to self-efficacy. columns Q2_1.1, Q2_2--8
cols = c("Q2_1.1", "Q2_2", "Q2_3", "Q2_4", "Q2_5", "Q2_6", "Q2_7", "Q2_8")
se = survey[, cols]
# See all unique values across all your SE columns
lapply(se[cols], unique)
$Q2_1.1
[1] "Neither agree nor disagree" "Somewhat agree"             "Somewhat disagree"          "Strongly agree"            

$Q2_2
[1] "Neither agree nor disagree" "Somewhat agree"             "Somewhat disagree"          "Strongly agree"             "Strongly Disagree"         

$Q2_3
[1] "Somewhat agree"             "Strongly agree"             "Neither agree nor disagree" "Somewhat disagree"         

$Q2_4
[1] "Neither agree nor disagree" "Somewhat agree"             "Strongly Disagree"          "Strongly agree"             "Somewhat disagree"         

$Q2_5
[1] "Somewhat agree"             "Strongly agree"             "Neither agree nor disagree" "Somewhat disagree"         

$Q2_6
[1] "Somewhat agree"             "Strongly agree"             "Somewhat disagree"          "Neither agree nor disagree"

$Q2_7
[1] "Strongly agree"             "Somewhat agree"             "Neither agree nor disagree" "Somewhat disagree"         

$Q2_8
[1] "Somewhat agree"             "Strongly agree"             "Neither agree nor disagree" "Somewhat disagree"         
# Numericize and average
efficacy_map = c("Strongly Disagree" = 1,
                 "Somewhat disagree" = 2,
                 "Neither agree nor disagree" = 3,
                 "Somewhat agree" = 4,
                 "Strongly agree" = 5)
se[cols] = lapply(se[cols], function(x) efficacy_map[x])
survey$self_efficacy = rowMeans(se[cols], na.rm = TRUE)
# Add column to timing_data for self_efficacy
full_df = merge(full_df,
                    survey[, c("PID", "self_efficacy")],
                    by = "PID",
                    all.x = TRUE)

Prep data from screener to put in analysis dataframe

screener = read.csv("patchwork_screener.csv")

# Chop off header in screener
screener = screener[3:nrow(screener), ]

# rename column to make easier to remember as predictor
screener = rename(screener, professional_YOE = Q11)
screener$professional_YOE = as.numeric(screener$professional_YOE)
screener$PID = as.factor(screener$PID)

# Singleton category is problematic, so we'll say unemployed participant is recently in their professional career
screener$professional_identity = ifelse(
  screener$professional_identity == "Unemployed",
  "Professional",
  screener$professional_identity
)
# Also combine to "grad student"
screener$professional_identity = ifelse(
  screener$professional_identity == "PhD" | screener$professional_identity == "Masters",
  "Grad",
  screener$professional_identity
)
screener$professional_identity = as.factor(screener$professional_identity)

Demographics of participants

# Find demographics: min max median age and YOE
screener$Age = as.numeric(screener$Age)
paste0("Median age: ", median(screener$Age, na.rm=TRUE))
[1] "Median age: 25"
paste0("Minimum age: ", min(screener$Age, na.rm=TRUE))
[1] "Minimum age: 18"
paste0("Maximum age: ", max(screener$Age, na.rm=TRUE))
[1] "Maximum age: 43"
paste0("Median YOE: ", median(screener$professional_YOE, na.rm=TRUE))
[1] "Median YOE: 2"
paste0("Minimum YOE: ", min(screener$professional_YOE, na.rm=TRUE))
[1] "Minimum YOE: 0"
paste0("Maximum YOE: ", max(screener$professional_YOE, na.rm=TRUE))
[1] "Maximum YOE: 15"
# Q13 is gender, Q16 is are you a student (answers: Yes, I am a Master's student, Yes, I am a Ph.D. student, No, Yes, I am undergraduate), Q10 is have you worked or are you currently working... with "Yes, I currently work at one" being the professional label
as.data.frame(table(screener$Q13))

RQ1a: Just run the models from APR, What is it Good For? (ICSE 2024)

# Note: these are not our final models for this RQ, and were not derived in a principled way. We're just seeing how the models from the paper fare for us.
correctness_model = glmer(correct ~ condition + (1 |PID) + (1 | bug), data = full_df, family = binomial)

summary(correctness_model)
Generalized linear mixed model fit by maximum likelihood (Laplace Approximation) ['glmerMod']

timing_model = lmer(time_minutes ~ condition + num_prev + (1 | PID) + (1 | bug), data = full_df)

summary(timing_model)

# Redo significance testing 
k1 <- glht(timing_model, mcp(condition="Tukey"))$linfct
k2 <- glht(timing_model, mcp(num_prev="Tukey"))$linfct
posthoc <- glht(timing_model, linfct = rbind(k1,k2))
summary(posthoc)

     Simultaneous Tests for General Linear Hypotheses

    (1 | bug), data = full_df)

Check predictors for collinearity issues and merge

# Q20 is IntelliJ IDEA IDE experience and Q21 is Java
# Subset screener to IntelliJ/Java experience
exp_vars = screener[, c("Q20", "Q21")]

exp_vars$Q20 = ifelse(screener$Q20 == "Not familiar at all",  1,
                      ifelse(screener$Q20 == "Slightly familiar",     2,
                             ifelse(screener$Q20 == "Moderately familiar",   3,
                                    ifelse(screener$Q20 == "Very familiar",         4,
                                           ifelse(screener$Q20 == "Extremely familiar",    5, NA)))))

exp_vars$Q21 = ifelse(screener$Q21 == "Not knowledgeable at all",  1,
                      ifelse(screener$Q21 == "Slightly knowledgeable",     2,
                             ifelse(screener$Q21 == "Moderately knowledgeable",   3,
                                    ifelse(screener$Q21 == "Very knowledgeable",         4,
                                           ifelse(screener$Q21 == "Extremely knowledgeable",    5, NA)))))

# estimates the correlation between two ordinal variables
polychoric(exp_vars)
Call: polychoric(x = exp_vars)
Polychoric correlations 
    Q20  Q21 
Q20 1.00     
Q21 0.63 1.00

 with tau of 
       1     2    3    4
Q20 -1.5 -0.59 0.21 0.98
Q21 -Inf -0.98 0.33 1.48
# Average into single component, since they covary highly enough
screener$java_intellij_experience = rowMeans(exp_vars, na.rm = TRUE)

# if number is low, we can count years of experience and java_intellij_experience as different predictors, not impacting model collinearity
cor(screener$professional_YOE, screener$java_intellij_experience,
    use = "complete.obs", method = "spearman")
[1] 0.1713451
# Look at correspondence between professional identity and YOE
# Boxplot: Years of Experience by Professional Identity
boxplot(professional_YOE ~ professional_identity, data = screener,
        main = "YOE by Professional Identity",
        xlab = "Professional Identity",
        ylab = "Years of Experience")


# Means of YOE within each category
# PhD and Masters have similar average YOE, but have different professional identities, so it may be valuable to include this as a predictor
aggregate(professional_YOE ~ professional_identity,
          data = screener,
          FUN = mean)

# Add column to full_df for professional YOE, java_intellij_experience, professional identity
full_df = merge(full_df,
                    screener[, c("PID", "java_intellij_experience", "professional_YOE", "professional_identity")],
                    by = "PID",
                    all.x = TRUE)

Subset dataframes for relevant analyses

gaze_valid_df = full_df[!is.na(full_df$Source.Code_fixation_count), ]

# boolean for whether they looked at the buggy method
gaze_valid_df$looked_at_buggy_method = ifelse(is.na(gaze_valid_df$ttff_buggy_method), FALSE, TRUE)

full_df_had_patch = full_df[full_df$had_patch, ]
# relevel bc no control
full_df_had_patch$condition = factor(full_df_had_patch$condition, 
                                       levels = c("correct", "overfitting"))
levels(full_df_had_patch$condition)
[1] "correct"     "overfitting"
gaze_valid_had_patch = full_df_had_patch[!is.na(full_df_had_patch$Source.Code_fixation_count), ]

RQ1a: How do patch suggestions affect time and accuracy of debugging sessions? (Section FIXME)

# And now, as determined by the DAG, participant insights, and prior literature, we define the maximal model
# FIXED EFFECTS:
# key players: treatment condition, professional YOE. these are the ones we'll try nested models for in LRT.
# intellij/java experience
# self-efficacy scores
# number of previous tasks, looking for ordering effects 

# Note: my intuition is that professional YOE/professional identity are too related as constructs for what we're trying to measure (latent expertise) to include both
# in the same model

# We would include treatment condition * professional YOE (hypothesis from participants: professionals might be less affected by wrong patches) and some other interaction effects, but we don't really have enough samples to be able to do this

# RANDOM EFFECTS:
# bug, PID
# optimizer maybe helps the convergence some because it goes for more iterations

full_correctness_model = glmer(correct ~ condition + professional_YOE
                              + java_intellij_experience + self_efficacy + num_prev
                              + (1 | PID) + (1 | bug), 
                              data = full_df, 
                              family = binomial)

# Do LRT to see whether condition, professional_YOE, and professional_YOE:condition improve the model
correctness_model_no_condition = glmer(correct ~ professional_YOE
                              + java_intellij_experience + self_efficacy + num_prev
                              + (1 | PID) + (1 | bug), 
                              data = full_df, 
                              family = binomial)
condition_LRT = anova(full_correctness_model, correctness_model_no_condition)

correctness_model_no_YOE = glmer(correct ~ condition
                              + java_intellij_experience + self_efficacy + num_prev
                              + (1 | PID) + (1 | bug), 
                              data = full_df, 
                              family = binomial)
YOE_LRT = anova(full_correctness_model, correctness_model_no_YOE)

# BH-correct for multiple comparisons in LRTs and present results
p_vals = c(
  condition    = condition_LRT$`Pr(>Chisq)`[2],
  YOE          = YOE_LRT$`Pr(>Chisq)`[2]
)

p_adjusted = p.adjust(p_vals, method = "BH")

# print both together for easy comparison
data.frame(
  predictor = names(p_vals),
  p_raw     = p_vals,
  p_adj     = p_adjusted
)

# LRT results: YOE does not improve the model
# Summarize the best model to report
summary(correctness_model_no_YOE)
Generalized linear mixed model fit by maximum likelihood (Laplace Approximation) ['glmerMod']
 Family: binomial  ( logit )
Formula: correct ~ condition + java_intellij_experience + self_efficacy +      num_prev + (1 | PID) + (1 | bug)

# Overall results:
# Correct patch suggestions predict solution correctness
# No evidence for overfitting patches making things worse

# Summary stats
summary(full_df$time_minutes)
   Min. 1st Qu.  Median    Mean 3rd Qu.    Max. 
  1.668  11.376  22.897  18.711  25.222  25.750 
# Look at distribution shape
# FIXME: survival analysis
hist(full_df$time_minutes, breaks = 30)


full_timing_model = glmer(time_minutes ~ condition + professional_YOE
                              + java_intellij_experience + self_efficacy + num_prev
                              + (1 | PID) + (1 | bug), 
                              data = full_df, 
                              family = Gamma(link="log"))

# Check residuals because of skewness 
residuals = resid(full_timing_model)
hist(residuals)

paste0("This is the skewness of the residuals: ", skewness(residuals))
[1] "This is the skewness of the residuals: -0.93085615325952" "This is the skewness of the residuals: 0.212301943902112"
# correcting for multiple comparisons for categorical predictors
k1 <- glht(timing_model, mcp(condition="Tukey"))$linfct
k2 <- glht(timing_model, mcp(num_prev="Tukey"))$linfct
posthoc <- glht(timing_model, linfct = rbind(k1,k2))
summary(posthoc)

     Simultaneous Tests for General Linear Hypotheses

Fit: lmer(formula = time_minutes ~ condition + num_prev + (1 | PID) + 
    (1 | bug), data = full_df)

Linear Hypotheses:
                           Estimate Std. Error z value Pr(>|z|)    
correct - control == 0      -5.8459     1.4320  -4.082  0.00026 ***
overfitting - control == 0  -4.7438     1.4126  -3.358  0.00454 ** 
overfitting - correct == 0   1.1022     1.4222   0.775  0.92071    
1 - 0 == 0                  -1.0461     1.4614  -0.716  0.93954    
2 - 0 == 0                  -1.8522     1.4405  -1.286  0.64373    
2 - 1 == 0                  -0.8061     1.4690  -0.549  0.97662    
---
Signif. codes:  0 ‘***’ 0.001 ‘**’ 0.01 ‘*’ 0.05 ‘.’ 0.1 ‘ ’ 1
(Adjusted p values reported -- single-step method)
model_parameters(timing_model)
# Fixed Effects 

Parameter               | Coefficient |   SE |         95% CI | t(119) |      p
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
(Intercept)             |       23.49 | 2.17 | [19.19, 27.79] |  10.82 | < .001
condition [correct]     |       -5.85 | 1.43 | [-8.68, -3.01] |  -4.08 | < .001
condition [overfitting] |       -4.74 | 1.41 | [-7.54, -1.95] |  -3.36 | 0.001 
num prev [1]            |       -1.05 | 1.46 | [-3.94,  1.85] |  -0.72 | 0.476 
num prev [2]            |       -1.85 | 1.44 | [-4.70,  1.00] |  -1.29 | 0.201 

# Random Effects 

Parameter           | Coefficient
---------------------------------
SD (Intercept: PID) |        0.00
SD (Intercept: bug) |        3.88
SD (Residual)       |        6.51
r2(timing_model)
Random effect variances not available. Returned R2 does not account for random effects.
# R2 for Mixed Models

  Conditional R2: NA
     Marginal R2: 0.143
Anova(timing_model)
Analysis of Deviance Table (Type II Wald chisquare tests)

Response: time_minutes
            Chisq Df Pr(>Chisq)    
condition 18.9763  2  7.575e-05 ***
num_prev   1.6638  2     0.4352    
---
Signif. codes:  0 ‘***’ 0.001 ‘**’ 0.01 ‘*’ 0.05 ‘.’ 0.1 ‘ ’ 1
# TODO: Are professionals more likely to edit the patch they were given? Are students more likely to overfit? (passes tests x is incorrect for a new column of “overfitting_fix”)

RQ1b: How do developer self-reports measure up to behavioral outcomes?

# Tables to make: think correct vs. actually correct, think patch correct vs. patch condition, would submit vs. actually correct
correct_tab = table(full_df$correct, full_df$think_correct)
patch_correct_tab = table(full_df_had_patch$condition, full_df_had_patch$think_patch_correct)
submit_correct_tab = table(full_df$would_submit, full_df$correct)

as.data.frame.matrix(correct_tab)
as.data.frame.matrix(patch_correct_tab)
as.data.frame.matrix(submit_correct_tab)

test_patch = chisq.test(patch_correct_tab, simulate.p.value = TRUE, B = 10000)
test_correct = chisq.test(correct_tab, simulate.p.value = TRUE, B = 10000)
test_submit = chisq.test(submit_correct_tab, simulate.p.value = TRUE, B = 10000)

test_patch

    Pearson's Chi-squared test with simulated p-value (based on 10000 replicates)

data:  patch_correct_tab
X-squared = 11.569, df = NA, p-value = 0.002
test_correct

    Pearson's Chi-squared test with simulated p-value (based on 10000 replicates)

data:  correct_tab
X-squared = 44.486, df = NA, p-value = 9.999e-05
test_submit

    Pearson's Chi-squared test with simulated p-value (based on 10000 replicates)

data:  submit_correct_tab
X-squared = 22.215, df = NA, p-value = 9.999e-05
# Standardized residuals (anything above 2 or below -2 is notable)
test_patch$stdres
             
                    IDK         N        YP
  correct      0.000000 -3.195048  3.146266
  overfitting  0.000000  3.195048 -3.146266
test_correct$stdres
       
               IDK          N         YP
  FALSE -0.1064785  6.4296785 -6.0848108
  TRUE   0.1064785 -6.4296785  6.0848108
test_submit$stdres
    
          FALSE       TRUE
  N   4.6381847 -4.6381847
  NQ -0.5603067  0.5603067
  YP -4.2995907  4.2995907
# Notably, participants are good at detecting if patches are correct and if their own fixes are, too

# We would like to see if professionals are better at determining the correctness of their fixes and the patch
professional_correct_tab = table(full_df$professional_identity, full_df$accurate_about_correctness)
professional_patch_correct_tab = table(full_df_had_patch$professional_identity, full_df_had_patch$accurate_about_patch_correctness)
professional_correct_tab
              
               FALSE TRUE
  Grad            21   53
  Professional     7   22
  Undergrad       11   13
professional_patch_correct_tab
              
               FALSE TRUE
  Grad            18   31
  Professional     6   13
  Undergrad        9    7
test_prof_accuracy = chisq.test(professional_correct_tab)
test_prof_patch_accuracy = chisq.test(professional_patch_correct_tab)

test_prof_accuracy

    Pearson's Chi-squared test

data:  professional_correct_tab
X-squared = 3.3574, df = 2, p-value = 0.1866
test_prof_patch_accuracy

    Pearson's Chi-squared test

data:  professional_patch_correct_tab
X-squared = 2.5373, df = 2, p-value = 0.2812
test_prof_accuracy$stdres
              
                    FALSE       TRUE
  Grad         -0.6726955  0.6726955
  Professional -0.8732358  0.8732358
  Undergrad     1.7836306 -1.7836306
test_prof_patch_accuracy$stdres
              
                    FALSE       TRUE
  Grad         -0.5664412  0.5664412
  Professional -0.7819324  0.7819324
  Undergrad     1.5442513 -1.5442513
# test also YOE
wilcox.test(professional_YOE ~ accurate_about_patch_correctness, 
            data = full_df_had_patch)

    Wilcoxon rank sum test with continuity correction

data:  professional_YOE by accurate_about_patch_correctness
W = 699.5, p-value = 0.19
alternative hypothesis: true location shift is not equal to 0
wilcox.test(professional_YOE ~ accurate_about_correctness, 
            data = full_df)

    Wilcoxon rank sum test with continuity correction

data:  professional_YOE by accurate_about_correctness
W = 1475.5, p-value = 0.2044
alternative hypothesis: true location shift is not equal to 0
# Verdict: we do not have significant evidence to suggest this is the case. May not be enough power.

ggplot(full_df, aes(x = would_submit, y = professional_YOE, 
                    fill = would_submit)) +
  geom_boxplot() +
  labs(title = "Would Submit vs. Professional Experience",
       x = "Would Submit", y = "Years of Experience") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "none")

RQ4: How do patch suggestions influence cognitive load during debugging? (Section FIXME)

# High level plan: do subjective reports from NASA-TLX, and then see if eye tracking metrics agree with the self-reports

# do model with self-reported predictors here
cognitive_load_model = lmer(cognitive_load ~ condition + professional_YOE
                              + java_intellij_experience + num_prev
                              + (1 | PID) + (1 | bug), 
                              data = full_df)

summary(cognitive_load_model)

# Redo significance testing
k1 <- glht(cognitive_load_model, mcp(condition="Tukey"))$linfct
k2 <- glht(cognitive_load_model, mcp(num_prev="Tukey"))$linfct
posthoc <- glht(cognitive_load_model, linfct = rbind(k1,k2))
summary(posthoc)
model_parameters(cognitive_load_model)
standardize_parameters(cognitive_load_model)

r2(cognitive_load_model)
Anova(cognitive_load_model)

# Gaze metrics related to cognitive load: average fixation duration
# related metric:
# avg_fixation_duration
fix_duration_model = glmer(avg_fixation_duration ~ condition + (1|PID) + (1|bug), 
                          data = gaze_valid_df,
                          family = Gamma(link="log"))
summary(fix_duration_model)
anova(fix_duration_model)

# TODO: do friedman test between average fixation duration before and after the patch

Testing hypotheses for RQ2: What debugging strategies and behaviors distinguish successful from unsuccessful debugging attempts? (Section FIXME)

Hypotheses derived from interview data

# TODO: figure out survivor analysis with anything where the outcome variable is time

# FIXME: broken, skewness won't work and might be masked by a different package (parameters)
# helper function to check if model residuals are normally distributed
check_residuals <- function(model) {
  model_name <- deparse(substitute(model))
  resids     <- residuals(model)
  skew       <- skewness(resids)
  
  par(mfrow = c(1, 2))
  hist(resids, main = paste("Residuals:", model_name), xlab = "Residuals")
  qqnorm(resids, main = model_name)
  qqline(resids, col = "red")
  par(mfrow = c(1, 1))
  
  cat(model_name, "- residual skewness:", round(skew, 3), "\n")
  if (abs(skew) > 0.5) {
    cat("  ^ consider a different model family or transform response var\n")
  }
}

# Hypothesis: people who look at the patch early on may be anchored to the patch's solution and may finish the task faster/more correctly
# expect condition:TTFF interaction ~ correctness and TTFF ~ time in glmer model
# TODO: make sure it's dropping all the relevant columns

# ttff_patch, avg_fixation_duration_patch (~ correctness)
ttff_patch_model = glmer(correct ~ ttff_patch + (1|PID) + (1|bug), 
                 data = timing_data, family = binomial)
summary(ttff_patch_model)

ttff_patch_model_time = lmer(time_minutes ~ ttff_patch + (1|PID) + (1|bug), 
                 data = timing_data)
summary(ttff_patch_model_time)
anova(ttff_patch_model_time)

# Hypothesis: Some developers described spending a while evaluating the patch and developing suspicion about it. Developers who look at the patch for longer or more carefully may be more likely to debug correctly or quickly. 
# fixation %/count and average fixation duration ~ correctness
# TODO: putting condition just for correctness since it appears to differ between overfitting/correct?
avg_dur_patch_model = glmer(correct ~ avg_fixation_duration_patch + condition + (1|PID) + (1|bug), 
                 data = timing_data, family = binomial)
summary(avg_dur_patch_model)

avg_dur_patch_model_time = lmer(time_minutes ~ avg_fixation_duration_patch + (1|PID) + (1|bug), 
                 data = timing_data)
summary(avg_dur_patch_model_time)
anova(avg_dur_patch_model_time)

# Hypothesis: Developers report triangulating multiple sources of information (tests, test output, code, patch, web-search) to debug, and prior work supports the idea that higher attention switching behavior correlates with accurate proof correction/problem-solving. Developers who exhibit higher attention switching behavior between AOIs may be more likely to debug correctly.
# look at number of cross-AOI switches / time ~ correctness
attention_switch_model = glmer(correct ~ attention_switching_rate + (1|PID) + (1|bug), 
                 data = timing_data, family = binomial)
summary(attention_switch_model)

attention_switch_model_time = lmer(time_minutes ~ attention_switching_rate + (1|PID) + (1|bug), 
                 data = timing_data)

# Hypothesis: Overwhelmingly, developers report having access to a patch speeding up fault localization. When developers have access to a patch, do they find the location of the bug faster and search more efficiently? 
# TTFF on buggy method ~ has_patch
# fixation rate ~ has_patch 
# check for subset of trials on Chart 12 -- this one had an overfitting patch that was not in the correct method
# First do logistic regression: did having a patch help people look at the buggy method more at all? 
# Then, try a survival analysis -- we have good reason to try this since we're trying to predict the time until an event occurs, with right-censored data
fl_model = glmer(looked_at_buggy_method ~ had_patch + (1|PID) + (1|bug), 
                 data = timing_data, family = binomial)
summary(fl_model)

# unfortunately, don't have enough data to just try this for chart12 since it embedded incorrect FL info

# Hypothesis: Along similar lines, developers also report having access to a patch narrowing their focus. When developers have access to a patch, do they look at a more narrow slice of a codebase or have a "pointier" distribution of attention?
# num_files_looked_at (should account for time confound), fixation_duration_entropy
# an offset just makes the coefficient 1, so creates assumption where there is a 1:1 relationship between the amount of time spent on task and the number of files you look at
num_files_m <- glmer(num_files_looked_at ~ had_patch + offset(log(time_minutes)) + (1|PID) + (1|bug), 
           data = timing_data, family = poisson)
summary(num_files_m)

entropy_m = lmer(fixation_duration_entropy ~ had_patch + (1|PID) + (1|bug), 
                 data = timing_data)
summary(entropy_m)

# TODO check all the residuals like so:
# check_residuals(entropy_m)

ART ANOVA for:

RQ2: What debugging strategies and behaviors distinguish successful from unsuccessful debugging attempts? (Section FIXME)

RQ3: How do patch suggestions influence developers’ debugging strategies and behaviors? (Section FIXME)

library("ARTool")
og_timing_data = timing_data
# TODO: determine whether '-' and 'Unknown/OOB' AOIs are MNAR by drawing pic. comment on this in paper

# Then, remove the "Patch" AOI for gaze distributions between correct vs. incorrect trials
# This is appropriate given our RQ targets how debugging behaviors /change/ in response to a patch, so we expect there to be some difference on the distribution
# over other AOIs

# TODO: also look at differences between when people got the problem correct/wrong just for when they had a patch 

# Find count and duration columns
fixation_count_cols    <- grep("fixation_count", names(timing_data), value = TRUE)
fixation_duration_cols <- grep("fixation_duration", names(timing_data), value = TRUE)

# Exclude other duration metrics from columns
# And also exclude patch fixations from the columns, since we're only interested in seeing how this affected other stages of debugging
fixation_duration_cols = fixation_duration_cols[fixation_duration_cols != "avg_fixation_duration"
                                                 & fixation_duration_cols != "avg_fixation_duration_patch"
                                                 & fixation_duration_cols != "fixation_duration_entropy"
                                                 & fixation_duration_cols != "Patch_fixation_duration"
                                                 & fixation_duration_cols != "OOB_fixation_duration"
                                                 & fixation_duration_cols != "X._fixation_duration"]

# Exclude missing data for AOIs or fixations outside of AOIs we consider
fixation_count_cols = fixation_count_cols[fixation_count_cols != "Patch_fixation_count"
                                          & fixation_count_cols != "X._fixation_count"
                                          & fixation_count_cols != "OOB_fixation_count"]

# fill 0s for NAs left in the fixation_counts/durations columns per row because ART can't work on NAs
timing_data[fixation_count_cols][is.na(timing_data[fixation_count_cols])] = 0
timing_data[fixation_duration_cols][is.na(timing_data[fixation_duration_cols])] = 0

# Create columns for the total counts and durations, then columns for the proportions of counts and durations per AOI
timing_data$total_fixation_count    <- rowSums(timing_data[, fixation_count_cols], na.rm = TRUE)
timing_data$total_fixation_duration <- rowSums(timing_data[, fixation_duration_cols], na.rm = TRUE)
timing_data[, paste0("prop_", fixation_count_cols)]    <- timing_data[, fixation_count_cols] / timing_data$total_fixation_count
timing_data[, paste0("prop_", fixation_duration_cols)] <- timing_data[, fixation_duration_cols] / timing_data$total_fixation_duration

# Subset to only participant-tasks where we had valid gaze data
timing_data <- timing_data[timing_data$total_fixation_count > 0, ]
print(nrow(timing_data))
table(timing_data$correct)
table(timing_data$had_patch)

run_art_analysis <- function(timing_data, prop_cols, group_var, metric) {

  # makes 'AOI' a column so there's a row for each one per participant-task
  timing_data_long <- reshape(
    timing_data[, c("PID", "bug", group_var, prop_cols)],
    varying   = prop_cols,
    v.names   = "proportion",
    timevar   = "AOI",
    times     = prop_cols,
    direction = "long"
  )
  
  rownames(timing_data_long) <- NULL
  
  # remove leading crap from AOI names
  timing_data_long$AOI <- gsub("prop_|_fixation_count|_fixation_duration", "", timing_data_long$AOI)

  timing_data_long$AOI            <- factor(timing_data_long$AOI)
  timing_data_long[[group_var]]   <- factor(timing_data_long[[group_var]])
  timing_data_long$PID <- factor(timing_data_long$PID)
  
# Visualization code 
# Compute mean proportion per AOI per group
means <- aggregate(proportion ~ AOI + get(group_var), data = timing_data_long, FUN = mean)
names(means)[2] <- group_var

# Reshape to wide for barplot
means_wide <- reshape(means,
  idvar     = "AOI",
  timevar   = group_var,
  v.names   = "proportion",
  direction = "wide"
)
rownames(means_wide) <- means_wide$AOI
means_wide$AOI <- NULL
# \\. is an escape sequence for . so it means a literal dot instead of a regex
colnames(means_wide) <- gsub("proportion\\.", "", colnames(means_wide))

# Plot
colors <- rainbow(nrow(means_wide))
barplot(as.matrix(means_wide),
  col     = colors,
  legend  = rownames(means_wide),
  beside  = FALSE,
  main    = paste0("Proportion of fixation ", metric, "s by AOI and ", group_var),
  xlab    = group_var,
  ylab    = "Mean proportion",
  args.legend = list(x = "topright")
)
  # Run the analysis
  formula <- as.formula(paste("proportion ~", group_var, "* AOI + (1|PID) + (1|bug)"))
  m <- art(formula, data = timing_data_long)
  return(list(model = m, anova = anova(m), data = timing_data_long))
}

# Seeing the difference in proportions between fixation counts and durations over AOIs given patch vs. no patch, correct vs. incorrect
count_prop_cols    <- paste0("prop_", fixation_count_cols)
duration_prop_cols <- paste0("prop_", fixation_duration_cols)

res1 <- run_art_analysis(timing_data, count_prop_cols,    "correct", "count")
res2 <- run_art_analysis(timing_data, count_prop_cols,    "had_patch", "count")
res3 <- run_art_analysis(timing_data, duration_prop_cols, "correct", "duration")
res4 <- run_art_analysis(timing_data, duration_prop_cols, "had_patch", "duration")


# Correct for multiple comparisons across interaction p-values
extract_pvalues <- function(res, analysis_name) {
  if (is.null(res$anova)) return(NULL)
  
  pvals <- res$anova$`Pr(>F)`
  effects <- rownames(res$anova)
  
  data.frame(
    analysis = analysis_name,
    effect   = effects,
    p_value  = pvals
  )
}

# Collect all p-values
pval_df <- rbind(
  extract_pvalues(res1, "count ~ correct"),
  extract_pvalues(res2, "count ~ had_patch"),
  extract_pvalues(res3, "duration ~ correct"),
  extract_pvalues(res4, "duration ~ had_patch")
)

# subset to interaction effects since those are our RQs and adjust
interaction_rows <- grepl(":", pval_df$effect)
pval_df$p_adjusted <- NA
pval_df$p_adjusted[interaction_rows] <- p.adjust(pval_df$p_value[interaction_rows], method = "BH")

# Print
print(pval_df)
